region_of_origin: Andhra Pradesh and Telangana, South India
food_subgroup: Mutton / Lamb curries — South Indian regional

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Gongura Mutton; closest defensible anchor is a lamb/mutton-based curry food entity. URI used is approximate.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dish — lamb/mutton, cooked with vegetables and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Gongura Mutton. A0CKP (cooked meat dish with vegetables) is the closest defensible category anchor.

main_ingredients: gongura (sorrel leaves / Hibiscus sabdariffa var. altissima)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Gongura Mutton is approximately 250 g, typically plated as a generous portion of thick sorrel-mutton curry alongside steamed rice at a South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Gongura Mutton is not listed as a single composite entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN data for cooked mutton curry and sorrel (gongura) leaves, weighted by typical recipe proportions (~60% mutton, ~20% sorrel paste, ~20% oil and spice base). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (USDA NDB 17174) proportionally adjusted. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available at composite level and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat and leafy green curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gongura Mutton. GI estimated at 25 (low) by analogy with high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat-and-leafy-green curry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content per serving (250 g) = 13.5 g; GL = (25 × 13.5) / 100 = 3.4. The high protein and fat content, combined with dietary fibre from sorrel leaves, substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from mutton
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, request reduced added salt during preparation, trim visible fat from the mutton, and monitor cumulative sodium intake from accompanying dishes such as rice and pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25), high protein (12.5 g/100 g), and dietary fibre from sorrel leaves (2.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gongura Mutton has a low GI and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.4), making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion the accompanying rice carefully and avoid high-GI side dishes to maintain overall meal glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton (~4.8 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (310 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Gongura Mutton in moderation (≤150 g per occasion), choose lean cuts of mutton, reduce added oil during preparation, and balance the meal with low-sodium, high-fibre accompaniments to mitigate saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of red meat; high acidity of sorrel leaves may affect some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption; the vitamin C and antioxidants in sorrel leaves are beneficial, but the high acidity may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals — consume occasionally and monitor response.

gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or elevated uric acid should avoid or strictly limit Gongura Mutton due to the high purine load from mutton, which can precipitate acute gout flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gongura Mutton (also known as Gongura Mamsam) is an iconic slow-cooked curry from the Andhra Pradesh and Telangana regions of South India. Its defining ingredient is gongura — the tangy sorrel leaf (Hibiscus sabdariffa var. altissima) — which imparts a sharp, acidic, and deeply savory character to the rich mutton gravy. The dish is prepared in two stages: bone-in or boneless mutton is slow-braised with ginger, garlic, green chilies, turmeric, and whole spices until tender and falling off the bone, while gongura leaves are separately sautéed with onions and red chilies before being blended into a coarse paste. The two components are then combined and simmered together until the oil separates and the gravy thickens into a dark, intensely flavored curry. The result is a bold, spicy, and tangy dish with a complex layering of heat, tartness, and umami from the meat. Nutritionally, Gongura Mutton provides approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with 12.5 g protein, 13.2 g fat, and 5.4 g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of iron (3.2 mg/100 g), zinc (3.8 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100 g), and vitamin A (125 mcg RAE/100 g), largely from the mutton and sorrel leaves respectively. The dietary fibre content (2.2 g/100 g) from the sorrel leaves contributes to a low glycemic index (~25) and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.4), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when paired with controlled portions of rice. However, the sodium content (310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g) make it a moderate-to-high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is traditionally served at Andhra festive meals and family lunches alongside steamed rice and a dollop of ghee. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with halal-certified mutton.

confidence_reason: Gongura Mutton is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked mutton curry and sorrel leaves, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked lamb/mutton. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct composite food entry in an approved source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17174), proportionally adjusted for the ~60% mutton fraction of the dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this traditional composite preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN tables or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gongura Mutton exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The portions array in the input contained four identical duplicate entries (250 g); deduplicated to a single 250 g default serving. 5. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified mutton. 6. Individuals with gout, high uric acid, or severe acid reflux (due to sorrel acidity) should avoid or limit this dish. 7. The input source 'Indian Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences' (phytochemical profile of Hibiscus cannabinus) is retained as a supporting reference for health suitability context only and is not used as a primary nutrient source.
